does: This paper develops a machine unlearning framework that reconstructs data distributions from model proxies to induce exact unlearning signals. Rather than directly modifying neural network parameters, the method infers the underlying data distributions with precision and uses these inferred distributions to guide the unlearning process. The authors derive theoretical bounds on the Kullback-Leibler divergence between their unlearned model and the ideal retrained model, establishing closeness guarantees under verifiable admissibility criteria. Experimental validation across three forgetting scenarios demonstrates that their approach produces classifiers closest to the ideal retrained model compared to existing unlearning methods.
connects: This work builds on the machine unlearning literature, which has grown in response to data privacy regulations and the need to remove the influence of specific data points from trained models. The paper engages with prior approaches that typically focus on parameter-level modifications and gradient-based techniques, positioning itself against these methods by arguing for a distribution-centric view. It draws on information-theoretic foundations, particularly using KL divergence to quantify model proximity, connecting to broader work on distribution reconstruction and model inference. The framework leverages recent advances in understanding how neural networks encode distributional information and extends connections between Bayesian methods and approximate inference relevant to privacy-preserving machine learning.
enables: This distribution-based paradigm for unlearning opens pathways toward more principled and theoretically grounded approaches to machine forgetting. By establishing closeness guarantees to the retrained model, the work enables practical certification of unlearning quality and could inform regulatory compliance mechanisms. The framework suggests future research into tighter reconstruction of data distributions from models, potentially extending to more complex scenarios with high-dimensional data or partial forgetting. It also points toward applications in federated learning and differential privacy integration, where understanding distributional shifts is critical. The theoretical insights could inspire new verification protocols for unlearning and improved methods for certifying that models have genuinely forgotten specific data influences.
